Irritable bowel syndrome, diagnosed at age 22

medications.frequency: as needed for acute anxiety, uses approximately twice per week

family_history: Mother has fibromyalgia and generalized anxiety disorder
Maternal aunt has epilepsy, diagnosed at age 30
No family history of stroke or movement disorders

social_history.drugs: Denies illicit drug use, denies marijuana use
social_history.living_situation: Lives with boyfriend in an apartment. Relationship described as supportive but strained by recent events
social_history.exercise: Previously active, jogging 3 times per week, stopped 6 weeks ago due to leg weakness
social_history.recent_stressors: Witnessed a motor vehicle accident 8 weeks ago in which a pedestrian was killed; has been having nightmares and flashbacks since

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert, oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Glasgow Coma Scale 15. Speech fluent, normal rate and prosody. Affect is anxious with intermittent tearfulness when discussing symptoms. Attention intact, able to recite months of the year backward. Memory 3/3 registration, 3/3 recall at 5 minutes. No evidence of cognitive impairment.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: CN II: Visual fields full to confrontation bilaterally. Pupils equal, round, reactive to light 3mm to 2mm bilaterally. Fundoscopic exam unremarkable, no papilledema. CN III/IV/VI: Extraocular movements intact without nystagmus. CN V: Sensation intact to light touch in all three divisions bilaterally. CN VII: Face symmetric at rest and with activation. CN VIII: Hearing grossly intact to finger rub bilaterally. CN IX/X: Palate elevates symmetrically. CN XI: Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius strength 5/5 bilaterally. CN XII: Tongue midline, no atrophy or fasciculations.
neurological_exam.motor: Bulk normal throughout. Tone normal in bilateral upper extremities. Right lower extremity demonstrates give-way weakness: initial resistance followed by sudden collapse of effort in hip flexion (2/5 on initial testing, 5/5 when distracted during seated hip flexion while donning shoes). Left lower extremity strength 5/5 throughout. Upper extremities 5/5 bilaterally. Hoover sign positive on the right: involuntary hip extension strength is full when testing left hip flexion against resistance, but voluntary right hip flexion is reportedly 2/5. No pronator drift. No muscle atrophy or fasciculations.
neurological_exam.sensory: Reports decreased sensation to light touch and pinprick over the entire right leg extending to the exact midline of the groin and abdomen, with a sharp demarcation at midline. This non-anatomical pattern does not follow any dermatomal or peripheral nerve distribution. Vibration sense intact at bilateral great toes, medial malleoli, and tibial tuberosities. Proprioception intact in all digits. Upper extremity sensation intact throughout.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Biceps 2+ bilaterally, triceps 2+ bilaterally, brachioradialis 2+ bilaterally, patellar 2+ bilaterally, Achilles 2+ bilaterally. Plantar responses flexor (downgoing) bilaterally. No clonus.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose testing intact bilaterally without dysmetria. Heel-to-shin testing performed normally on the left; on the right, patient demonstrates inconsistent performance with exaggerated swaying but no true dysmetria. Rapid alternating movements intact bilaterally.
neurological_exam.gait: Patient uses a wheelchair and states he cannot walk due to right leg weakness. When assisted to stand, demonstrates a dragging gait pattern with the right leg held stiffly behind. However, on observational testing when distracted, he was noted to bear full weight on the right leg momentarily while adjusting his hospital gown. No Trendelenburg sign. Romberg testing: dramatic swaying in all directions without falling, with self-correction at extreme sway angles (functional Romberg).
neurological_exam.additional: During the examination, two brief episodes of bilateral upper extremity shaking lasting approximately 45 seconds and 30 seconds were witnessed. During these events, the patient's eyes were tightly closed (forced eye closure with resistance to passive opening), he demonstrated asynchronous, irregular, side-to-side head movements, and the tremor was variable in frequency (2-6 Hz) and amplitude. The tremor entrained to examiner-imposed rhythm during contralateral hand tapping. He remained responsive to verbal commands throughout, stating 'I can't stop it.' No post-event confusion. These features are consistent with psychogenic non-epileptic seizures (PNES).

patient.chief_complaint: Right leg weakness and shaking episodes for the past 6 weeks
patient.history_present_illness: Mr. Miguel Santos is a 22-year-old right-handed woman who presents to the neurology service as a direct admission from his primary care physician's office for evaluation of progressive right leg weakness and recurrent episodes of whole-body shaking over the past six weeks. The patient reports that approximately eight weeks ago he witnessed a severe motor vehicle accident in which a pedestrian was struck and killed. He was deeply affected by this event and has since experienced nightmares, flashbacks, and heightened anxiety. Two weeks after the accident, he noticed that his right leg began to feel 'heavy and weak,' initially intermittent but progressively worsening to the point where he now requires a wheelchair for mobility. He describes the weakness as affecting his entire right leg from the hip downward, and he also notes decreased sensation over the right leg and right lower abdomen. He denies any back pain, bowel or bladder dysfunction, or left-sided symptoms. Concurrently, he has developed episodes of shaking involving his arms and sometimes his whole body, occurring two to four times daily, each lasting 30 seconds to 3 minutes. During these episodes he reports being aware of his surroundings but unable to control the movements. He denies tongue biting, urinary incontinence, or post-event confusion. His boyfriend has witnessed multiple episodes and describes them as variable in intensity and pattern, noting that they sometimes occur during stressful conversations. He reports that he has never injured himself during an episode and that he can respond to questions throughout. He was seen in an outside emergency department three weeks ago where a CT head was unremarkable and he was discharged with a referral to neurology. His primary care physician, noting the progressive nature and functional impairment, arranged for direct inpatient admission for comprehensive evaluation. The patient is worried that he may have multiple sclerosis or epilepsy and is seeking answers. He acknowledges significant psychological distress since the accident but does not believe his symptoms are related to stress.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 10 Hz alpha rhythm, symmetric, well-modulated, reactive to eye opening
background.anterior_rhythms: Low-amplitude beta activity, diffusely distributed, likely medication-related (benzodiazepine use)
background.sleep_features: Stage II sleep captured with vertex waves, sleep spindles, and K-complexes. No abnormal sleep architecture

artifacts.description: Intermittent muscle artifact, does not obscure underlying activity
Two clinical events captured with diffuse movement artifact; EEG obscured by artifact during movement but no epileptiform correlate before, during, or after events when interpretable

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Performed for 3 minutes. Mild diffuse symmetric slowing, returned to baseline within 30 seconds. No epileptiform discharges provoked.
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: Performed at flash frequencies 1-30 Hz. Normal photic driving response. No photoparoxysmal response.

eeg.impression: Normal awake and asleep EEG. Two clinical events characterized by bilateral upper extremity shaking were captured during this recording. The EEG demonstrated NO epileptiform correlate during either event. The background remained normal throughout, with no post-event slowing or suppression. No interictal epileptiform abnormalities were identified. Clinical correlation recommended.
eeg.limitations: Movement artifact during clinical events partially obscured the EEG record, but sufficient channels remained interpretable to confirm absence of ictal EEG correlate.

additional_observations: No white matter lesions or demyelinating plaques
No mass lesion or space-occupying abnormality
Normal brainstem, cerebellum, and craniocervical junction
Normal cortical ribbon, no cortical atrophy
No vascular malformation or aneurysm on MRA
mri.impression: Normal brain MRI with gadolinium. No evidence of demyelinating disease, stroke, mass lesion, or structural abnormality to explain the patient's symptoms. Normal MRA of the circle of Willis. Clinical correlation recommended.

Additional.test: Post-event prolactin (drawn 25 minutes after witnessed episode)

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 10 Hz alpha rhythm, symmetric, well-regulated, reactive
background.sleep_features: Normal sleep architecture captured including stages N1, N2, and brief N3. No epileptiform abnormalities in sleep.

artifacts.description: Five clinical events captured over 72-hour monitoring period, each with diffuse movement artifact. No electrographic seizure correlate identified during any event.

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Performed daily. No epileptiform abnormalities provoked.
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: Performed daily. Normal photic driving. No photoparoxysmal response.
activating_procedures.sleep_deprivation: One night of partial sleep deprivation performed. No epileptiform abnormalities provoked.

output.impression: 72-hour continuous video-EEG monitoring captured five habitual clinical events. All events demonstrated bilateral, asynchronous, variable-frequency limb shaking with preserved awareness and forced eye closure. None of the five events had any EEG correlate: no ictal discharge, no post-ictal slowing, no post-ictal suppression. The interictal EEG was entirely normal with no epileptiform abnormalities. Clinical correlation recommended.
output.limitations: None. Habitual events were captured and the study is considered diagnostic.

results.title: Cognitive behavioral therapy for functional neurological disorder: A randomized controlled trial
results.summary: CODES trial: CBT specifically designed for PNES did not significantly reduce seizure frequency compared to standard medical care at 12 months, but did improve secondary outcomes including psychological distress, psychosocial functioning, and quality of life.

results.title: Consensus recommendations for the diagnosis and treatment of functional neurological disorder
results.summary: Expert consensus recommends: (1) diagnosis using positive clinical signs (Hoover sign, tremor entrainment), not by exclusion; (2) clear and empathetic communication of the diagnosis; (3) multidisciplinary treatment including physiotherapy, psychology/psychiatry, and occupational therapy; (4) avoidance of unnecessary investigations that reinforce illness behavior.

results.title: Physical rehabilitation for functional motor symptoms: a systematic review
results.summary: Systematic review supporting the role of specialized physical therapy for functional motor disorder. FND-informed physiotherapy focuses on movement retraining, distraction techniques, and graded activity rather than strengthening exercises.

output.summary: Current evidence supports a multidisciplinary approach to FND treatment. Key components include: (1) Diagnosis based on positive neurological signs, not exclusion; (2) Clear, empathetic communication of the diagnosis as the first therapeutic step; (3) CBT adapted for FND/PNES addresses psychological comorbidity and illness beliefs; (4) FND-informed physical therapy using motor retraining and distraction techniques; (5) Treatment of psychiatric comorbidity (depression, anxiety, PTSD); (6) Avoiding unnecessary investigations and iatrogenic harm. Prognosis is better with earlier diagnosis, younger age, shorter symptom duration, and engagement in psychological treatment.

output.proposed: Sertraline 150 mg daily (dose increase from 100 mg)
interactions: Sertraline + Lorazepam: Minor interaction. SSRIs may potentiate sedative effects of benzodiazepines. Monitor for excessive sedation.
warnings: Monitor for serotonin syndrome symptoms if combining with other serotonergic agents
Black box warning: increased suicidality risk in patients under 25 years old; patient is 26 but close monitoring is recommended
Dose increase may initially worsen anxiety before improvement
output.formulary_status: Preferred formulary, no prior authorization required
alternatives: Venlafaxine 75-150 mg daily (SNRI alternative with evidence for PTSD)
Fluoxetine 20-60 mg daily (FDA-approved for depression and may help with PTSD)

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Functional neurological disorder (conversion disorder) with weakness and psychogenic non-epileptic seizures

differential.key_distinguishing: PNES features present: forced eye closure, variable frequency and amplitude tremor, entrainment to external rhythm, preserved awareness during events, no post-ictal confusion, normal post-event prolactin, no EEG correlate during captured events.

Normal brain MRI without white matter lesions or enhancing plaques. Normal spine MRI without cord lesions. Normal inflammatory markers. Sensory deficit follows non-anatomical midline splitting pattern rather than dermatomal distribution.

Young patient without vascular risk factors. Subacute progressive onset rather than acute. Weakness demonstrates give-way pattern with positive Hoover sign. Normal brain MRI with no DWI restriction. Symptoms do not follow a vascular territory.
No fatigable weakness pattern, no ptosis, no diplopia, no bulbar symptoms. Weakness is give-way type with inconsistency between voluntary and involuntary movements. Normal EMG/NCS.

differential.diagnosis: Lumbar radiculopathy or cauda equina syndrome
differential.key_distinguishing: No back pain, no bowel or bladder dysfunction. Sensory deficit has midline splitting pattern rather than dermatomal distribution. Normal spine MRI. Normal EMG/NCS. Reflexes preserved and symmetric.

optimal_actions.action: Perform detailed neurological examination with attention to positive functional signs (Hoover sign, tremor entrainment, give-way weakness, inconsistency between voluntary and involuntary movements)
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Positive Hoover sign, give-way weakness, tremor entrainment, midline splitting of sensory loss, functional Romberg

optimal_actions.action: Obtain brain MRI with gadolinium to rule out structural lesions, demyelination, and stroke
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal brain MRI, no structural abnormality

optimal_actions.action: Obtain routine or video-EEG to capture habitual events and confirm absence of epileptiform correlate
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal EEG with no ictal correlate during captured clinical events, confirming PNES

optimal_actions.action: Obtain comprehensive laboratory studies including CBC, BMP, TSH, ESR/CRP, B12, post-event prolactin
optimal_actions.expected_finding: All labs normal including normal post-event prolactin (distinguishing PNES from epileptic seizure)

optimal_actions.action: Communicate the diagnosis to the patient in a clear, empathetic, non-dismissive manner, explaining that FND is a real neurological condition involving abnormal nervous system functioning
Refer to psychiatry for comprehensive evaluation and treatment of psychiatric comorbidities (PTSD, depression, anxiety)
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Identification of PTSD, depression, and anxiety as comorbid and contributing conditions

optimal_actions.action: Refer to psychology for cognitive behavioral therapy specifically designed for FND/PNES
Refer to FND-informed physical therapy focusing on movement retraining and distraction-based techniques rather than traditional strengthening
Consider optimization of antidepressant medication (sertraline dose increase or switch) for treatment of comorbid depression and PTSD
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Safe to increase sertraline dose; alternatives include paroxetine or venlafaxine for PTSD

optimal_actions.action: Arrange multidisciplinary outpatient follow-up with neurology, psychiatry, psychology, and physical therapy

critical_actions: Recognize positive functional neurological signs on examination (Hoover sign, give-way weakness, tremor entrainment) as diagnostic evidence for FND, not a diagnosis of exclusion
Obtain EEG (preferably video-EEG) to capture events and confirm absence of epileptiform correlate, establishing PNES diagnosis
Obtain brain MRI to rule out structural causes while recognizing that FND is diagnosed by positive signs
Communicate the diagnosis empathetically and clearly, explaining that symptoms are real and treatable
Do NOT prescribe antiepileptic drugs for psychogenic non-epileptic seizures
Refer for appropriate multidisciplinary treatment including CBT and FND-informed physiotherapy
contraindicated_actions: Prescribing antiepileptic drugs for PNES (no efficacy, risk of side effects, reinforces incorrect diagnosis)
Telling the patient her symptoms are 'all in her head' or 'not real' (damaging to therapeutic alliance and factually incorrect)
Performing unnecessary invasive procedures such as lumbar puncture when there is no clinical indication
Ordering excessive additional investigations that reinforce illness behavior and delay appropriate treatment
Discharging without a clear treatment plan or follow-up for FND management
Prescribing benzodiazepines as standing medication for the shaking episodes (reinforces seizure-like illness behavior)
key_reasoning_points: FND should be diagnosed by POSITIVE clinical signs (Hoover sign, give-way weakness, tremor entrainment, functional Romberg), not merely by excluding organic disease
The Hoover sign (involuntary hip extension is full strength during contralateral hip flexion testing, while voluntary hip flexion is weak) is a reliable positive sign for functional leg weakness
Tremor entrainment (the tremor changes frequency to match examiner-imposed rhythm during contralateral tapping) is a positive sign for functional tremor
Midline splitting of sensory loss (sharp demarcation at the anatomical midline) is a positive sign for functional sensory disturbance, as organic lesions do not respect the exact midline
Normal post-event prolactin level supports PNES over epileptic seizure, as prolactin is typically elevated 10-20 minutes after generalized tonic-clonic seizures
Forced eye closure during events is characteristic of PNES; epileptic seizures typically feature open eyes
The temporal relationship between the traumatic event (witnessed fatal MVA) and symptom onset is a common pattern in FND, with psychological trauma serving as a precipitant
Normal MRI and labs are expected in FND but alone do not make the diagnosis; the diagnosis requires identification of positive functional signs
FND is a real neurological condition, not malingering or factitious disorder; patients are not intentionally producing symptoms

clinical_pearls: FND is the second most common reason for outpatient neurology consultation and should be diagnosed by positive signs, not by exclusion
The Hoover sign has high sensitivity and specificity for functional leg weakness and should be tested in all patients presenting with lower extremity weakness
Psychogenic non-epileptic seizures account for approximately 20-30% of patients referred to epilepsy monitoring units
Early diagnosis and appropriate communication of the diagnosis are the most important prognostic factors in FND
Psychiatric comorbidity (especially depression, anxiety, PTSD, and history of childhood trauma) is common but not universal in FND; absence of psychiatric history does not exclude the diagnosis
